_meta.title: Parallel Patterns — Map, Reduce, Pipeline, Scan

questions.question: What distinguishes a parallel map from a parallel reduce?
options: Map applies a function to each element independently; reduce combines all elements with an associative operator
Map requires synchronization between threads; reduce is embarrassingly parallel
Map can only be done sequentially; reduce is always parallel by nature
Map and reduce are the same pattern under different names
questions.explanation: Map applies a function f to each element with no data dependences between elements — it is embarrassingly parallel (W=n, T=O(1)). Reduce combines all elements with an associative operator ⊕, requiring a tree combine where partial results depend on their children (W=n−1, T=O(log n)). The two patterns are often chained (map-reduce) but have fundamentally different work–span profiles.

questions.question: What is the span (critical path length) of a parallel tree reduction on n elements?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
questions.explanation: A parallel tree reduction organizes the associative combine into a binary tree of depth log₂n. The root cannot be computed until both subtrees are complete, so the critical path (span) is O(log n). The total work remains O(n) — the tree does not reduce the number of operations, only the depth of dependences.

questions.question: Why is a parallel prefix sum (scan) harder to parallelize than a map?
options: The scan operator is non-associative and cannot be reordered
Each output depends on all previous inputs, creating an apparent sequential dependence chain
Scan requires O(n²) memory, making parallelization memory-bound
Scan can only be implemented recursively, which prevents parallel execution
questions.explanation: In an inclusive scan, output[i] = x₀ ⊕ x₁ ⊕ ... ⊕ xᵢ. Every output depends on every preceding input — naive evaluation looks serial. Parallel scan algorithms (Hillis-Steele, Blelloch) overcome this by reusing partial sums across outputs, achieving O(log n) span. This cleverness is why the scan is considered a fundamental parallel primitive (it is not obvious that it can be parallelized at all).

questions.question: A pipeline has three stages with per-item latencies of 4ms, 7ms, and 2ms. What is the maximum steady-state throughput?
options: 500 items/s (limited by the fastest stage at 2ms)
~142 items/s (limited by the slowest stage at 7ms)
~77 items/s (limited by the total latency of 13ms)
250 items/s (limited by the first stage at 4ms)
questions.explanation: In steady state, the pipeline's throughput is 1 / max(L₁, L₂, ..., Lₖ) — the slowest stage is the bottleneck. Here max latency = 7ms, so throughput = 1/0.007 ≈ 142 items/s. The total latency per item is 13ms (4+7+2), but latency and throughput are distinct metrics. Improving any non-bottleneck stage has zero effect on throughput. This is Amdahl's law applied to pipelines.

questions.question: What is the Map-Reduce parallel pattern?
options: A way to store data in a database
Map applies a function to each element independently; Reduce combines results using an associative operation
A compression algorithm
A networking protocol
questions.explanation: Map applies a transformation to each element in parallel (no dependencies). Reduce combines results using an associative operation (sum, max, etc.) — this can be done in a tree pattern for O(log n) parallel time. Map-Reduce scales from multicore CPUs to clusters of thousands of machines.
